perturbation.original_text: 14.3 CHOICE OF LAW; ARBITRATION; VENUE. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California, USA, excluding conflict of laws provisions and excluding the 1980 United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods. Any disputes arising out of this Agreement shall be resolved by binding arbitration in accordance with the then-current commercial arbitration rules of the American Arbitration Association ("RULES"). The arbitration shall be conducted by one (1) arbitrator appointed in accordance with the Rules in San Francisco County, California. A judgment upon the award may be entered in any court having jurisdiction of the parties, including without limitation the courts in San Francisco, California. The non-prevailing party in the arbitration shall pay all fees and charges of the American Arbitration Association; each party, however, shall be responsible for the payment of all fees and expenses connected with the presentation of its respective case.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.3 CHOICE OF LAW; ARBITRATION; VENUE. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California, USA, excluding conflict of laws provisions and excluding the 1980 United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods. Any disputes arising out of this Agreement shall be resolved in accordance with processes the parties find agreeable. The non-prevailing party in the arbitration shall pay all fees and charges of the American Arbitration Association; each party, however, shall be responsible for the payment of all fees and expenses connected with the presentation of its respective case.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the binding arbitration clause from the main body of the dispute resolution section to a less prominent position, suggesting flexibility in dispute resolution instead of mandatory arbitration. The core statement about binding arbitration has been eliminated, therefore arbitration is not mandatory. Disputes can be resolved in accordance with processes the parties find agreeable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires arbitration agreements to be clear and explicit. By replacing the explicit arbitration terms with a vague statement about 'agreeable processes,' the contract now contradicts the requirement for a clear agreement to arbitrate. It also introduces ambiguity, potentially undermining the enforceability of any arbitration decision, as the initial agreement to arbitrate isn't clearly defined.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281.1. For the purposes of this article, any request to arbitrate made pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 1299.4 shall be considered as made pursuant to a written agreement to submit a controversy to arbitration. (Added by Stats. 2000, Ch. 906, Sec. 1. Effective January 1, 2001.)
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated 'binding arbitration,' but the changed text replaces this with a vague statement about 'processes the parties find agreeable.' This contradicts the requirement for a clear agreement to arbitrate, as stated in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses arbitration agreements, it doesn't explicitly state that arbitration agreements must be 'clear and explicit.' However, the removal of the term 'binding arbitration' is a significant departure from a mandatory process, creating ambiguity about dispute resolution. The modification removes a core legal obligation to engage in binding arbitration. This makes the contract structurally flawed and creates a legal contradiction because the new language weakens and contradicts the original intent to resolve disputes through binding arbitration, thereby undermining the enforceability of any potential arbitration decision.

perturbation.original_text: 9 LIMITATION OF LIABILITY. EXCEPT FOR LIABILITY ARISING UNDER SECTIONS 2.7, 8, 10 AND 12.3 OF THIS AGREEMENT, IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY'S LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT OR THE USE OR PERFORMANCE OF THE SOFTWARE EXCEED THE TOTAL AMOUNT ACTUALLY PAID BY CORIO HEREUNDER FOR THE TRANSACTION WHICH THE LIABILITY RELATES TO DURING THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRIOR TO THE CAUSE OF 13 ACTION FIRST ARISING, EVEN IF IT IS A CONTINUOUS ONE, OR IN THE AGGREGATE, WITH RESPECT OF ALL CLAIMS ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, THE TOTAL AMOUNT ACTUALLY PAID BY CORIO UNDER THIS AGREEMENT TO CHANGEPOINT. EXCEPT FOR LIABILITY ARISING UNDER SECTIONS 2.7, 8, 10 AND 12.3 OF THIS AGREEMENT, IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY HAVE ANY LIABILITY TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY LOST PROFITS OR COSTS OF PROCUREMENT OF SUBSTITUTE GOODS OR SERVICES, OR FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTIAL, SPECIAL OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES HOWEVER CAUSED AND UNDER ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY AND WHETHER OR NOT SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGE. THE PARTIES AGREE THAT THIS SECTION 9 REPRESENTS A REASONABLE ALLOCATION OF RISK.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.8 SUBCONTRACT. Changepoint understands and agrees that Corio shall solely direct the provision of Corio Services and may subcontract certain portions of the operations of the Corio Services to third parties at any time during the term of the Agreement. Corio shall not sublicense its distribution license rights under Section 2.5. EXCEPT FOR LIABILITY ARISING UNDER SECTIONS 2.7, 8, 10 AND 12.3 OF THIS AGREEMENT, IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY'S LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT OR THE USE OR PERFORMANCE OF THE SOFTWARE EXCEED THE TOTAL AMOUNT ACTUALLY PAID BY CORIO HEREUNDER FOR THE TRANSACTION WHICH THE LIABILITY RELATES TO DURING THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRIOR TO THE CAUSE OF 13 ACTION FIRST ARISING, EVEN IF IT IS A CONTINUOUS ONE, OR IN THE AGGREGATE, WITH RESPECT OF ALL CLAIMS ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, THE TOTAL AMOUNT ACTUALLY PAID BY CORIO UNDER THIS AGREEMENT TO CHANGEPOINT. EXCEPT FOR LIABILITY ARISING UNDER SECTIONS 2.7, 8, 10 AND 12.3 OF THIS AGREEMENT, IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY HAVE ANY LIABILITY TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR ANY LOST PROFITS OR COSTS OF PROCUREMENT OF SUBSTITUTE GOODS OR SERVICES, OR FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTIAL, SPECIAL OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES HOWEVER CAUSED AND UNDER ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY AND WHETHER OR NOT SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGE. THE PARTIES AGREE THAT THIS SECTION 9 REPRESENTS A REASONABLE ALLOCATION OF RISK.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the Limitation of Liability to the Subcontracting section, which is a general administrative section. This makes it less visible and harder to find for parties reviewing potential liabilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1668 states that contracts are illegal if they exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent. While limitation of liability clauses are generally enforceable, they cannot shield a party from gross negligence or intentional misconduct. By burying the clause in an administrative section, it becomes more difficult for a party to understand the limitations and could be misconstrued as an attempt to limit liability for actions that are non-excludable under California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code Section 1668 by placing the limitation of liability clause in an administrative section, potentially obscuring it. According to the law explanation, this could be misconstrued as an attempt to limit liability for actions that are non-excludable under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that contracts that aim to exempt someone from responsibility for their own fraud or willful injury are against the policy of the law. By hiding the liability clause, it makes it more difficult for a party to understand the limitations, which creates a structural flaw and a potential legal contradiction. The placement weakens the enforceability and transparency of the liability limitations, aligning with the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section.
